content.assessment: This proposal keeps the existing selection architecture intact and layers technical adjustments (exam redesign, SES proxies, interview cap, outreach mandates) onto a centralized algorithmic admissions system that already exists (F-live-implementation_planning-06), which is a meaningful implementation advantage: the delivery infrastructure and central authority to enact rule changes are already consolidated. However, the proposal offers no explicit sequencing, piloting, or monitoring design, and it must operate within a system beset by an unresolved measurement-trust crisis: disputed statistics on performance and finance outcomes (F-live-implementation_planning-09) would compromise any feedback loop meant to detect whether coaching-resistant exams or SES proxies are working or being gamed. The workforce capacity crisis (F-live-implementation_planning-07) is largely orthogonal to this proposal since it does not require new teacher deployment, which is a relative strength versus structural alternatives.
opportunities: Centralized control over curriculum, hiring, and finance (F-live-implementation_planning-06) could be used to mandate uniform outreach/registration support to disadvantaged schools with lower administrative friction than a decentralized system would allow
strengths: Leverages an already-centralized admissions algorithm and unified national authority, reducing the number of actors who must coordinate to implement rule changes (F-live-implementation_planning-06)
Does not require teacher redeployment or workforce expansion, sidestepping the acute teacher-capacity crisis that constrains more structural reforms (F-live-implementation_planning-07)
threats: Absence of monitoring infrastructure to detect elite adaptation to new exam formats means erosion of gains could go undetected for years

Persistent disputes over education statistics (F-live-implementation_planning-09) could be exploited by opposing actors to contest whether the reform is achieving its stated goal, stalling adaptation cycles
weaknesses: No specified piloting phase, monitoring protocol, or adaptation mechanism is described for testing whether SES proxies or exam redesign actually reduce coaching-driven sorting before full rollout
Contested and disputed baseline statistics on performance and outcomes undermine the independent feedback loop needed to evaluate whether the reform is working (F-live-implementation_planning-09)
